title: Indent and Auto-Indent
subtitle: {key:>>} indents. {key:<<} dedents. {key:=}{motion} re-indents using the language indenter.
summary: >{motion} indents a range one shiftwidth. <{motion} dedents. ={motion} runs the language's indent function. Doubled forms (>>, <<, ==) act on the current line.

blocks.text: {key:>}, {key:<}, and {key:=} are all *operators*. They take a motion or text object, like {key:d} and {key:y}. {key:>}{key:i}{key:p} indents the inner paragraph. {key:=}{key:a}{key:{} re-indents the brace block.

blocks.title: >, <, = Are Operators — That's the Whole Insight
blocks.text:
Once you internalize that {key:>}, {key:<}, and {key:=} are operators (verbs), every motion you know becomes an indenter:

  • {key:>}{key:G} — indent from here to end of file.
  • {key:>}{key:i}{key:{} — indent inside the next brace block.
  • {key:>}{key:%} — indent everything between this brace and its match.
  • {key:=}{key:a}{key:p} — auto-indent the surrounding paragraph.
  • {key:=}{key:G} — auto-indent the rest of the file.

The **shift width** (how much {key:>} indents) comes from `shiftwidth`. With `expandtab` set, tabs are expanded to spaces.

{key:=} delegates to whatever `indentexpr` or `cindent` is configured. For most languages, ftplugin sets this up automatically — pressing {key:=}{key:i}{key:p} on Python or JavaScript does the right thing.
In *visual mode*, {key:>} and {key:<} indent the selection. With a count: {key:3}{key:>} indents three times. {key:.} repeats the last indent.

blocks.title: Indent operators are leading-whitespace rewrites
blocks.text: {key:>} and {key:<} are line-transform operators: for each affected line they recompute leading whitespace using `shiftwidth` and `expandtab`, then rewrite that prefix. After the rewrite, the cursor lands on the first non-blank of the first affected line — a deterministic landing rule that lets you chain {key:>}{key:.}{key:.} reliably.
